metadata.description: A short tutorial showing how to add accessibility to your forms
metadata.objectives: Show users how to use a Drupal Website
metadata.author: Peter K. Johnson
metadata.creator: Created by Course Genie, licensed to South Central College.

newpage.para: Several elements are available that will make your forms more accessible to all users.
Labeling form objects help screen readers determine what is what on your form.

heading.b: Helping Screen Readers Determine Which text goes with which label

newpage.para: The label is a signpost. It tells the browser, "This text box is called firstName.''
Here's what the code looks like:
This creates an unambiguous (non-confusing) link between the text that displays on screen "First Name" and the input textbox which has the attribute id="firstName".

An added benefit to using a label is a large area is established on screen. This is especially useful when using radio buttons and check boxes. The user can click anywhere on the label to select the item.

heading.b: id=" " means there will be only one per page

newpage.para: The key to all of this is the id=" " attribute. Each id=" " attribute must be unique for that page. This allows the label to know exactly which item it is attached to.

heading.b: Use the <legend> to chunk your form into groups

newpage.para: If you are presented with a list of 50 seemingly unrelated checkboxes on a survey it can be very daunting and will frighten most users away.
You can use the <fieldset> element to chunk the form into groups.
For example, you could break the 50 questions into 5 clearly groups that relate to each other. This will greatly increase the usability of the page for all users.

heading.b: Use the <legend> element to identify each fieldset.

newpage.para: Here is an example of the <fieldset> and <legend> elements.
Here is the code:

paneltext.para:
<fieldset>
<legend>My Radio Buttons</legend>
<input type="radio" id="rad0" name="rad" />
<label for="rad0">Option 0</label>
<input type="radio" id="rad1" name="rad" />
<label for="rad1">Option 1</label>
<input type="radio" id="rad2" name="rad" />
<label for="rad2">Option 2</label>
. . .
</fieldset>

newpage.para: CSS can be used to change the outline or character formatting. You can either use the in-line style or use an id or class designation in the <head> section of the page.
Notice the how the ~  non-breaking spaces were used to separate the items making it clear which circle goes with which label onscreen.
CCI Space was designed to help you communicate. The more everyone uses it the more useful it will become.

subheading.b: Need help or have a suggestion?

newpage.para: Just send me a quick note using the Contact page.
Peter Johnson
August 2006
Revised November 2006

para.b: This completes this tutorial: ASP.NET Forms

newpage.para: Written by Peter K. Johnson - October 2006
